The apostle, after finishing the section of his letter where he argues and proves various doctrines which are practically applied, now urges important duties based on gospel principles. He asked the Romans, as his brothers and sisters in Christ, by the mercies of God, to present their bodies as a living sacrifice to Him. This is a powerful appeal. We receive from the Lord every day the fruits of his mercy. Let us dedicate ourselves; all that we are, all that we have, all that we can do: and after all, what return is it for such great blessings? It is pleasing to God: a reasonable service, which we are able and ready to explain, and which we understand. Conversion and sanctification are the renewing of the mind; a change, not of our basic nature, but of our character. The progress of sanctification, dying to sin more and more, and living to righteousness more and more, continues this renewal work until it is perfected in glory. The greatest obstacle to this renewal is conformity to the ways of the world. Be careful not to make plans for happiness as though it were found in worldly things, which quickly pass away. Do not adopt the customs of those who live according to their physical desires and focus on earthly matters. The work of the Holy Spirit first begins with understanding, then moves to the will, affections, and actions, until there is a complete change within a person into the likeness of God, in knowledge, righteousness, and true holiness. Therefore, being godly means giving ourselves completely to God.

Pride is a sin that comes naturally to us; we need caution and protection against it. All believers make up one body in Christ, who is the Head of the body and the central point of their unity. Within this spiritual body, some are suited for and called to one type of work; others for another type of work. We should do all the good we can for each other and for the common benefit. If we carefully considered the abilities we have and how much we fail to use them properly, it would humble us. But just as we must not be proud of our talents, so we must also be careful that, under a guise of humility and self-denial, we are lazy in working for the good of others. We should not say, 'I am nothing, therefore I will sit still and do nothing,' but rather, 'I am nothing on my own, and therefore I will dedicate myself to the utmost, with the strength of Christ’s grace.' Whatever our gifts or circumstances may be, let us strive to use ourselves humbly, diligently, cheerfully, and simply; not seeking personal credit or profit, but the good of others, for this world and the one to come.

The professed love that Christians have for each other should be sincere, free from deceit, and without empty or misleading compliments. Relying on God’s grace, they must hate and fear all evil, and love and enjoy whatever is kind and helpful. We must not only do what is good, but we must hold onto it. All our duties toward one another are summed up in one word: love. This describes the love of parents for their children; which is more tender and natural than any other; unforced, unrestrained. And love for God and people, with enthusiasm for the gospel, will make a wise Christian diligent in all his worldly business and in developing superior skills. God must be served with sincerity, under the influence of the Holy Spirit. He is honored by our hope and trust in him, especially when we rejoice in that hope. He is served not only by working for him, but also by remaining still and quiet when he calls us to suffer. Patience for God’s sake is true devotion. Those who rejoice in hope are likely to be patient in times of trouble. We should not be cold in our prayers, nor quickly become weary of them. Not only must there be kindness to friends and family, but Christians must not harbor anger toward their enemies. It is a false kind of love that rests on words of kindness while our brothers and sisters are in need, and it is within our power to help them. Be ready to welcome those who do good; as the occasion arises, we should welcome strangers. Bless, and do not curse. This means complete goodwill; not blessing people when you pray for them and cursing them at other times, but blessing them always and never cursing at all. True Christian love will lead us to share in each other’s sorrows and joys. Strive as much as possible to agree on the same spiritual truths; and even if we fall short of that, let us still agree in affection. View worldly wealth and status with a holy disdain. Do not value them; do not be attached to them. Accept the place God has put you in through his care, whatever it may be. Nothing is beneath us except sin. We will never truly humble ourselves toward others while we have an inflated opinion of ourselves; therefore, that pride must be overcome.

Since people became enemies of God, they have been very quick to become enemies with each other. And those who embrace religion should expect to encounter enemies in a world whose approval rarely aligns with Christ’s. Do not repay evil for evil. That is a brutish response, fitting only for animals that are unaware of any being above them or of life after death. Furthermore, strive and be careful to do what is admirable and respectable, and what presents religion favorably to everyone you interact with. Seek out things that promote peace; if it’s possible without offending God and violating your conscience. Do not seek revenge yourself. This is a difficult lesson for our sinful nature, so a remedy against it is added. Give way to anger; when someone's passion is high and the force is strong, let it pass; lest it be made to rage more against us. The line of our duty is clearly marked out, and if our enemies are not softened by persistent kindness, we are not to seek vengeance; they will be consumed by the fiery wrath of God, to whom vengeance belongs. The final verse suggests something that the world does not easily understand: that in all conflict and argument, those who take revenge are defeated, and those who forgive are victorious. Do not be overcome by evil. Learn to thwart malicious plans against you, either by changing them or by preserving your own peace. He who has this control over his spirit is better than the mighty. God’s children may be asked whether it is not sweeter to them than all earthly good that God enables them through his Spirit to feel and act in this way.
